posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked:
• 2014 Domaine Pierre Guillemot Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru Narbantons - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Savigny-lès-Beaune 1er Cru (3/17/2017)
    Man is this good. Pop and Pour and revisited for three days. This is dark, but limpid, red. It is pretty and fresh, with a tart, cool, and juicy deep and dark red cherry attack. The tannins come across smooth, with a hint of wood box and a little stemmy perception of clove/spice. I like this and will buy more. Great premier cru Burgundy for under 40 beans.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Levi Pinsky
posts.cooked: Good note. Had the 2013 the other night and really enjoyed it. More accessible at this point than the Serpentieres. Pure pleasure right now.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: Thanks, this was my first Guillemont! I got a mixed case based on a rec from a trusted palate. Glad I did.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: This is the top Burgundy producer in my cellar by number of bottles. Martin Steinley fixed me up with a mixed case of 2006-2009 and I’ve been buying all the subsequent vintages. I really like them and the price is right.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I visited last year to review the 2015s and was very impressed. We ended up embarking upon a spontaneous blind tasting of Serpentières back to 1947 (the latter a revelation). The family seem to have been producing very serious, ageworthy wines for quite some time. Don’t miss the 2015s.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: I love this! Thanks for the note.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked:
Levi Pinsky:

Good note. Had the 2013 the other night and really enjoyed it. More accessible at this point than the Serpentieres. Pure pleasure right now.

Good to get some confirmation on this. Can recall not deriving much from the 13’ Serp on release. Perhaps worse was a 14’ Mongeard-Mugneret Narbantons had a couple months ago.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Charlie, your note nails this wine, in pretty much any vintage - “limpid,” “fresh,” “cool,” “juicy,” and “spice.” Narbantons is the juiciest/fleshiest of all Guillemot cuvees and, as a result, it shows the best among them when young. Serpentieres and Gravains (as the vines gain some age) are more taut, lean and linear, and similarly limpid, fresh and cool as you describe. IMO, Guillemot remains one of Burgundy’s great values. $40 gets one a bottle that looks pretty smart around $100 bottles from further north. (As indicated above, I sell Guillemot.)

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: Thanks a ton Martin. Great breakdown on the wines. I have a mixed case of the other two reds you mention and a white. I really want to try one of each for a baseline… Then it off to collecting and drinking more. I agree with your price point breakdown.

posts.name: P@u1_M3nk3s
posts.cooked:
The 2010 Savignys I’ve had have been ridiculously good, and the 2010 Bourgogne was a great quaffer at $18.
I really like Guillemot but sometimes they aren’t so hot. If the general vintage had problems Guillemot tends to have them as well. 2003 was flabby and 2004 disgusting. That being said, in leaner vintages they make nice wines, such as in 2001.

posts.name: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note. I like his Savignys, though I find them hard in the early years and in need of considerable time.

I scored a case of his 2010 Corton le Rognet for $46 per a while back, but have never had it. Does anyone have any experience?
posts.display_username: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: That’s a screamin’ deal, Drew. Guillemot’s Corton is a lean, taut, muscular wine. That 2010 will need a lot of time, but it will be really good.

posts.name: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K
posts.cooked: Thanks, Martin. Yep - was pleased to get that. They will sleep for a long, long time.
posts.display_username: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K
